did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Multi-view Filtered Transfer Learning (MFTL) method, which is a deep learning approach involving deep neural networks and adversarial domain adaptation, to perform skin lesion classification (specifically Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis) on the ISIC 2017 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel Multi-view Filtered Transfer Learning (MFTL) method for skin lesion classification, presents its architecture and algorithms, and reports original experimental results (including AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity) on the ISIC 2017 dataset. It includes sections on Introduction, Related Works, Methodology, Results, and Conclusion, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Skin Lesion Classification by Multi-View Filtered Transfer Learning
brief_summary: This study addresses the scalability limitations of deep neural networks in skin cancer diagnosis by proposing a Multi-view Filtered Transfer Learning (MFTL) method for classifying Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis. The approach utilizes adversarial domain adaptation to distill valuable knowledge from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain, specifically the ISIC 2017 dataset. MFTL employs a source-sample-correlation mining algorithm based on Wasserstein distance to evaluate the importance of source images and neglect negative samples. The method extracts discriminative features from multiple image views using several feature extractors and classifiers to strengthen representation capability. Extensive experiments on the ISIC 2017 dataset demonstrate that the proposed MFTL method achieves an average AUC of 91.8%. These results indicate that the method effectively solves skin lesion classification tasks with outstanding extensibility compared to existing supervised and domain adaptation models.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task on the ISIC 2017 dataset. The paper reports an average AUC of 87.9% for Melanoma classification.
One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task on the ISIC 2017 dataset. The paper reports an average AUC of 95.8% for Seborrheic Keratosis classification.
Mentioned in the Introduction as part of non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) statistics, but not used as a target class in the experiments.
Mentioned in the Introduction as part of non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) statistics, but not used as a target class in the experiments.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification tasks for Melanoma (MM) and Seborrheic Keratosis (SK) using the ISIC 2017 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant or specific lesion types) using a Multi-view Filtered Transfer Learning (MFTL) network. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and AUC (Area Under the Curve).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A popular skin lesion dataset released in the 2017 International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI), hosted by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains dermatoscopic images for ternary classification of Malignant Melanoma (MM), Seborrheic Keratosis (SK), and Benign Nevi (BN). In this study, it served as the unlabeled target domain.

use.training: Used as the target domain for domain adaptation; specifically, 600 testing images were utilized for experiments.
use.validation: Validation images were part of the dataset structure but not explicitly used for model validation in the described transfer learning setup (target domain was unlabeled).
use.testing: 600 testing images were used to conduct experiments and evaluate performance.
use.other: Served as the unlabeled target domain for the multi-view filtered transfer learning method.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, released in the ISIC 2018 challenge (task 3). It contains 10,015 skin lesion images across 7 categories. In this study, specific classes were selected to serve as the labeled source domain.

use.training: Served as the labeled source domain. Melanoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma images were selected to match the target classes.
use.other: Used as the source domain for transfer learning; knowledge was distilled from this labeled source to the unlabeled target domain (ISIC 2017).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the multi-view correlation between source samples and the target domain for source-sample-correlation mining and filtering negative samples.
To calculate True Positives (TP), False Negatives (FN), True Negatives (TN), and False Positives (FP) for deriving classification metrics.
To visualize classification performance and calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) as a gold indicator for model ranking capability.
To evaluate the individual contributions of the multi-view weighing representation module and the filtered domain adaptation module by comparing performance with and without these components.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using classification metrics (AUC, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity) and ranks, without statistical significance testing.

architecture_used.pretraining: Networks were pre-trained on the source domain (HAM10000 dataset) before being further trained on the proposed multi-view filtered transfer learning framework.

architecture_details.backbone_details: ResNet-50 was employed as the architecture for feature extractors (F and F^t) excluding the last fully connected layer.
architecture_details.multi_view_structure: The framework integrates three image views: raw image view, texture view (using LBP algorithm), and shape view (using wavelet transformation).
architecture_details.feature_extractors: M individual Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) feature extractors (F_m) were used, one for each view.
architecture_details.classifiers: A sequence of M classifiers (C_m) was deployed after each feature extractor. The last fully connected layer with softmax from ResNet-50 was used as the framework for classifiers.
architecture_details.domain_adaptation: Adversarial domain adaptation module using adversarial discriminators (D_m) to minimize Wasserstein distance between target and source feature distributions.
architecture_details.weighing_strategy: Multi-view weighing component calculates weights (w_m) for each view based on source-sample-correlation mining using Wasserstein distance, summing to 1.

loss_functions: Wasserstein distance minimization for feature extractors in adversarial domain adaptation (L_Ft)
Filtered cross-entropy loss (L_filtered) using selected source samples
training_details.optimization: Adversarial training strategy for discriminators and feature extractors; gradient penalty deployed for trainable parameters in discriminators.

classification_or_output_head.details: The last fully connected layer with softmax from ResNet-50 served as the classifier framework. Final prediction is a weighted sum of individual view predictions: Result(x_jt) = sum(w_m * C_m(F_m(V_m(x_i_t))))

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation via rotating, flipping, and crop operations
Multi-view extraction: Raw image view, texture view using Local Binary Patterns (LBP) algorithm, and shape view using wavelet transformation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotating, flipping, and crop operations were applied to training images.

conclusions: The proposed Multi-view Filtered Transfer Learning (MFTL) method effectively solves Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis classification tasks with outstanding extensibility compared to existing supervised and domain adaptation models.
MFTL achieves an average AUC of 91.8% on the ISIC 2017 dataset, which is higher than the top three winners of the ISIC 2017 challenge and recent state-of-the-art methods.

The method demonstrates absolute advantages over conventional neural networks (ResNet50, RAN50, SENet50) while saving annotating costs by utilizing only labeled source domain data (HAM10000) to predict unlabeled target domain images (ISIC 2017).

The multi-view weighing representation module and filtered domain adaptation module are critical components; ablation studies show that removing filtering or using single views reduces performance (e.g., average AUC drops from 91.8% to lower values).

Existing domain adaptation methods for skin lesion classification often achieve unsatisfactory recognition accuracy due to the large distribution gap between source and target domains, which MFTL addresses by distilling valuable source samples and neglecting negative samples.
The source-sample-correlation mining algorithm based on Wasserstein distance effectively measures the contribution of each source sample to the target domain, improving the transfer learning process.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Melanoma vs. Seborrheic Keratosis) and does not address other critical skin cancer types like Basal Cell Carcinoma or Squamous Cell Carcinoma, limiting its generalizability to broader clinical scenarios.

The method relies on transfer learning from the HAM10000 dataset as the source domain; performance is contingent on the distributional similarity between HAM10000 and the target ISIC 2017 dataset, which may not hold for other populations or imaging devices.

The target domain (ISIC 2017) was treated as unlabeled during the training process to simulate real-world scalability, meaning the model was not supervised by ground truth labels from the target dataset during adaptation.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance improvements over baseline models.
The paper does not report external validation on independent datasets outside of ISIC 2017, raising concerns about potential dataset bias and generalizability to unseen clinical environments.

The source-sample-correlation mining uses Wasserstein distance to filter negative samples; the paper notes that Euclidean distance was found more suitable than Cosine similarity for this specific task, suggesting sensitivity to the choice of distance metric.
The model was trained for only 25 epochs, which may be insufficient to fully converge or generalize compared to longer training regimes used in other deep learning studies.

The study claims to save annotating costs by using only labeled source data, but this advantage is offset by the complexity of the adversarial domain adaptation framework and the need for careful source domain selection.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study employs a transfer learning framework where a labeled source domain (HAM10000) is used to train a model for an unlabeled target domain (ISIC 2017). Specifically, Melanoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma images from HAM10000 serve as the source domain, while the testing subset of ISIC 2017 (containing Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis classes) serves as the target domain for evaluation. The model is trained using labeled source data and evaluated on the held-out test images of the target dataset. No separate validation set was explicitly used for model selection during the transfer learning phase described; the target domain's validation split was not utilized for training or validation, only the test split was used for final evaluation.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset (source domain) and tested on the ISIC 2017 dataset (target domain). This constitutes cross-dataset validation as the training and testing data come from different named cohorts with different distributions.

training.counts: Subset of 10,015 images; specifically Melanoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma classes were selected to match target classes.
training.notes: Used as the labeled source domain for transfer learning. Knowledge was distilled from this domain to the unlabeled target domain.

validation.notes: The paper does not report using a specific validation set for model selection/tuning in the transfer learning setup. The target domain's validation split was not used.

testing.counts: 600 testing images (117 Melanoma, 90 Seborrheic Keratosis, 393 Benign Nevi). Note: The binary classification tasks focused on Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis.
testing.notes: Used as the unlabeled target domain for evaluation. The model was tested on the 600 testing images provided by the ISIC 2017 challenge.

external_testing.notes: Served as the external/unseen target domain relative to the source domain (HAM10000) during training.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The baseline models (ResNet50, RAN50, SENet50) were likely pre-trained on ImageNet, though the paper focuses on the transfer from HAM10000 to ISIC 2017. The MFTL method initializes weights by transferring pre-trained ResNet50 parameters.
Used as the source domain for transfer learning. Labeled data from HAM10000 was used to adapt the model to the target domain.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation was applied to training images via rotating, flipping, and crop operations.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: The target domain (ISIC 2017) provided a fixed test set of 600 images. The source domain (HAM10000) provided labeled data for training. No random train/test split within a single dataset was performed for the final evaluation; it was a cross-dataset evaluation.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: The ISIC 2017 testing set was treated as unseen/unlabeled during the training phase (transfer learning) and used for final evaluation.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results on the Melanoma class from the ISIC 2017 test set.
Results on the Seborrheic Keratosis class from the ISIC 2017 test set. Average AUC across both tasks was 91.8%.

metrics.auc: 85.6% (Original Image Only), 88.5% (Single View Average), 83.2% (No Filtering), 86.8% (Cosine Similarity)

dataset_level_results.notes: Ablation results on the ISIC 2017 test set comparing different components of the MFTL method.

cross_dataset_experiments.notes: The model was trained on HAM10000 (source) and tested on ISIC 2017 (target). This is a cross-dataset experiment.

generalizability_notes: The study is limited to binary classification (Melanoma vs. Seborrheic Keratosis) and does not address other skin cancer types.
Performance depends on the distributional similarity between HAM10000 and ISIC 2017; generalizability to other populations or imaging devices is not validated.
No external validation on independent datasets outside of ISIC 2017 was performed.
The target domain was treated as unlabeled during training, simulating real-world scalability but limiting supervised fine-tuning on the target.
information_not_reported: Specific counts of training images used from HAM10000 (only total dataset size and selected classes are mentioned).
Validation set usage details (no explicit validation set for model selection was reported).
Patient-level separation details (image-level split is implied by the dataset structure).
